- [Man]: Presents a strict 'side_profile' view pointing 'right'. This indicates it is positioned laterally (side-ways).
- [Woman]: Presents a strict 'side_profile' view pointing 'left'. This indicates it is positioned laterally (side-ways).

=== Absolute Spatial Relationships (Camera Perspective) ===

- [Horizontal]: Trolley is to the LEFT of Man.

- [Depth]: Man is CLOSER to the camera than Woman.
- [Horizontal]: Woman is to the LEFT of Man.
- [Depth]: Box is CLOSER to the camera than Man.
- [Horizontal]: Box is to the LEFT of Man.
- [Depth]: Box is CLOSER to the camera than Woman.
- [Horizontal]: Woman is to the LEFT of Box.

=== Pairwise Geometric & Orientation Deductions ===
- [Alignment/Rotation]: One is 'diagonal_front' and the other is 'side_profile'. They are NOT PARALLEL; the diagonal object is ROTATED/ANGLED forming an oblique angle relative to the other.
- [Alignment/Rotation]: One is 'diagonal_front' and the other is 'side_profile'. They are NOT PARALLEL; the diagonal object is ROTATED/ANGLED forming an oblique angle relative to the other.
- [Alignment/Rotation]: One is 'diagonal_front' and the other is 'frontal'. They are NOT PARALLEL; the diagonal object is ROTATED/ANGLED forming an oblique angle relative to the other.
- [Parallelism]: Both 'Man' and 'Woman' present a strict 'side_profile'. Their longitudinal axes are PARALLEL.
- [Relative Direction]: 'Man' points 'right' while 'Woman' points 'left'. They are pointing in OPPOSITE / DIVERGING directions.
- [Perpendicularity]: 'Man' is 'side_profile' and 'Box' is 'frontal'. This strict geometric discrepancy dictates they are PERPENDICULAR (at ~90-degree angle) to each other.
- [Perpendicularity]: 'Woman' is 'side_profile' and 'Box' is 'frontal'. This strict geometric discrepancy dictates they are PERPENDICULAR (at ~90-degree angle) to each other.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- When two objects of the same category are at different depths, their apparent sizes in the image differ due to perspective projection. The object closer to the camera appears larger even if both are physically identical. Before concluding a true size difference, mentally relocate both objects to the same depth plane.
- A single pairwise size difference between two instances does not justify a global conclusion such as 'all different' or 'all same.' Aggregate the comparison across every visible instance and choose the answer that best describes the overall distribution, such as 'mostly similar.'
- Apparent occlusion of a large distant object by a small nearby object does not mean the small object could physically cover or replace the large one. A flower pot in the foreground may visually overlap a tree in the background, yet the flowers could only cover a tiny branch of that tree in reality.
- When comparing a loop, chain, ring, or surrounding structure to the object it encircles, distinguish inner radius from outer radius and from enclosing span. A chain running around the outside of a wheel has a larger effective radius than the wheel itself.
- For items displayed together in retail, craft, or decorative contexts, the most reliable size comparison is within the same design family. Cross-design comparisons can mislead because shape, color, and decoration differences bias perceived size independently of true dimensions.

7.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- An object is in stable equilibrium when the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls within its base of support (the area of contact with the supporting surface). A wider base of support and a lower center of gravity both increase stability. Conversely, a narrow base with a high center of gravity is the least stable configuration.
- Objects placed in normal orientations on flat, stable surfaces are stable by default. A cup on a table, books in a neat stack, a vase on a shelf — these are all stable arrangements. Instability should only be predicted when there is a clear physical reason: an extremely narrow base, significant surface tilt, a precision-balanced arrangement, or an applied external force of sufficient magnitude.
- There is a critical distinction between precision-balanced structures and normally placed objects. Precision-balanced structures — such as stones carefully stacked on points or edges, card houses, or objects balanced on a knife-edge — are at the tipping point and can be toppled by even tiny forces (a drop of water, a gust of air, a slight vibration). Normally placed objects sitting on their natural flat base have comfortable stability margins and require forces comparable to their own weight to displace.
- The effect of a perturbation on a structure depends on the ratio of the perturbation's force or mass to the structure's own mass. When the perturbing agent's mass is less than about 1% of the structure's mass and the structure is in normal placement or anchored, the perturbation has no significant effect. For example, a bird landing on a lamp post, a raindrop falling on a table, or an insect crawling on a book cause no instability.
- When the perturbing agent's mass is a significant fraction (10% or more) of the structure's mass, noticeable effects are possible. For example, a child climbing a lightweight ladder may cause it to shift or tip; a heavy bag placed on the edge of a small table may cause tilting.
- Humans have active balance recovery ability through reflexive muscle adjustments. A child whose hand is released on a trail will likely slip momentarily but then recover balance, not fall uncontrollably — unless the surface is very steep, very slippery, or the child is on an unstable platform. Adults have stronger recovery ability than children.
- When one object is removed from a multi-object arrangement, only objects that directly depended on the removed object for support are affected. Objects with their own independent contact with the supporting surface remain stable.
- Anchored or very heavy structures are effectively immovable by everyday forces. Lamp posts cemented in the ground, wall-mounted televisions, utility poles, fire hydrants, and large trees are not affected by moderate wind, rain, birds landing, or light impacts.
- When asked 'which object is the least stable', the answer is the object with the smallest base of support relative to its height. A thin wooden spoon standing on its handle end (line contact) is less stable than a dried flower bunch spread at its base. The assessment should focus on the geometry of the base, not on how fragile or delicate the object appears.
- On tilted surfaces, objects with curved contact surfaces (balls, cylinders lying sideways, circular frames) tend to roll even at very small tilt angles of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases remain stationary due to friction up to approximately 15 degrees on rough surfaces or 5 degrees on smooth surfaces.
- When a person sits on a heavy piece of furniture such as a bench, chair, or bed, the furniture remains stable. Heavy or wide-based objects on the furniture also remain in place. Only loose, lightweight objects with small contact areas — unsecured pillows, loose papers, a light blanket draped over the edge — might be displaced by the person's movement.
- When a child jumps on a bed or sofa, the most likely displaced items are loose lightweight objects on the surface — pillows, stuffed animals, light blankets — that can bounce off due to the elastic surface's response. The bed frame itself and wall-mounted items are unaffected.
- Force applied above an object's center of gravity tends to cause tipping or toppling. Force applied below the center of gravity tends to cause sliding. Heavy furniture resists both; light furniture is susceptible to both.
- In most everyday scenes, the correct prediction for 'what happens if X is slightly disturbed' is 'nothing significant changes'. Dramatic collapses, chain-reaction falls, or catastrophic instability are rare in normal household arrangements and should only be predicted when the physical setup clearly warrants it — such as precision-balanced structures or heavily overloaded shelves.

11.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- Domain Rules (Proximity Gradients) ---
- Positions described relative to the 'frame' or 'image boundaries', such as the center of the frame or the top of the image, refer exclusively to 2D Cartesian coordinates (X/Y axes) and must not be evaluated using 3D depth relative to the camera (Z axis).
- When two objects differ simultaneously in their horizontal positioning and their depth relative to the camera, their spatial alignment relative to each other is strictly diagonal, regardless of whether their front planes are oriented parallel to the camera.
- When assessing the closest or farthest depth of specific anatomical parts of a subject viewed in a side or diagonal profile, protruding extremities such as the nose, beak, or leading shoulder represent the extreme depth limits and supersede broader anatomical regions like the entire head or body.
- In evaluating the greatest proximity to a specific subject, direct physical contact or visual overlap by foreground environmental elements, such as surrounding grass, leaves, or branches, often takes precedence over biologically attached body parts that are positioned further back in the 3D space.
- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- Handedness depends on the geometry of grasping and use, not on whether the object appears on the left or right side of the image.
- A tool with a symmetric shaft and no side-specific handle, trigger, or guard is typically neutral with respect to left-handed or right-handed use.
- Long linear tools such as mops, brooms, poles, and sticks are usually hand-neutral unless they contain a visible asymmetric grip structure.
- A true left- or right-hand preference usually arises from asymmetric handle placement, unilateral guards, offset grips, or directional controls.
- The tilt direction of a tool does not by itself imply a preferred carrying hand.
- The fact that a tool is placed to the left of another object does not imply that it is easier to grasp with the left hand.
- When no clear side-specific affordance is visible, a neutral interpretation is more physically grounded than forcing a left or right bias.
- Questions about carrying convenience refer to the accessibility of the grasp region rather than the semantic identity of nearby objects.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Proximity Gradients) ---
- Positions described relative to the 'frame' or 'image boundaries', such as the center of the frame or the top of the image, refer exclusively to 2D Cartesian coordinates (X/Y axes) and must not be evaluated using 3D depth relative to the camera (Z axis).
- When two objects differ simultaneously in their horizontal positioning and their depth relative to the camera, their spatial alignment relative to each other is strictly diagonal, regardless of whether their front planes are oriented parallel to the camera.
- When assessing the closest or farthest depth of specific anatomical parts of a subject viewed in a side or diagonal profile, protruding extremities such as the nose, beak, or leading shoulder represent the extreme depth limits and supersede broader anatomical regions like the entire head or body.
- In evaluating the greatest proximity to a specific subject, direct physical contact or visual overlap by foreground environmental elements, such as surrounding grass, leaves, or branches, often takes precedence over biologically attached body parts that are positioned further back in the 3D space.
- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

55.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.

61.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a room, person, or task depends on proportional compatibility, not just absolute dimensions. A chandelier that is physically large may still be too small for a grand hall, while a small lamp may be perfectly appropriate for a reading nook.
- Furniture, lighting fixtures, signs, and decorative centerpieces are normally chosen to be proportionate to the room they occupy. When a question asks whether a fixture is 'appropriate' or 'needs to be larger,' it is testing awareness of room-to-object scale matching, not just whether the fixture exists.
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- For questions about seating capacity, stacking fit, or carrying capacity of containers, reason about functional volume and arrangement geometry rather than only comparing one linear dimension such as width or height. Two medium tables together can seat as many people as one large table if each table's perimeter provides sufficient chair positions.
- When objects are stacked, their combined height may or may not match the interior height of a container. If individual items are shorter than the container, stacking more layers can eventually fill the space, but only if the question specifies enough layers. Do not assume a single additional stack equals the remaining gap.

62.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- When counting objects that block a door, exit, or path, enumerate every distinct physical item in the approach corridor individually before summing. Common blockers include hand trucks, mop buckets, brooms, cones, chairs, boxes, and cleaning equipment. Do not stop at the one or two most obvious items; systematically scan the entire access zone from left to right and floor to ceiling.
- A door's access zone extends beyond the door frame itself: it includes the full swing arc of the door panel, the corridor a person must walk through to reach the door, and any area needed to grip and pull or push the handle. Any object—even a small cone, bucket, or leaning broom—that falls within this extended zone counts as a blocker.
- For large-count obstruction questions (e.g., 10+ items blocking a door), build a structured inventory by scanning the scene in spatial order (left-to-right, bottom-to-top). Assign each visually distinct item a number. Count stacks of identical items (e.g., water bottle packs, cardboard boxes) as separate objects when they are individually distinguishable. Verify the final count against the answer options before committing.
- On multi-lane roads and highways, a vehicle in one lane does not have a direct path to vehicles in the opposing lane or on a different level if a median barrier, guardrail, jersey wall, or grade separation intervenes. 'Direct path' means continuous same-surface traversal without crossing physical dividers. Opposing traffic flow alone is insufficient to block a path, but a concrete median is.
- When identifying which specific object obstructs a target (e.g., which drawer obstructs the glasses), trace an imaginary access line from the user's approach direction to the target object. The obstructing object is the first item that intersects this access line. For vertically stacked furniture like shelves or drawer units, an open or protruding drawer on the floor above can intrude downward into the access corridor of the shelf below.
- Human unaided vertical reach is approximately 2.0–2.3 meters for an average adult. In warehouse or industrial shelving, the first (ground-level) shelf is typically reachable; shelves from the second tier upward are generally unreachable without a ladder or lift. A ceiling-mounted object such as a chandelier or overhead pipe is always inaccessible by normal human reach. When the question asks which floors are unreachable, err toward including more floors as unreachable rather than fewer.
- When a question asks about the reason for inaccessibility and multiple plausible reasons are listed, select the most specific and visually verifiable reason rather than defaulting to 'all of the above.' For example, if no staircase, ladder, or elevator is visible connecting ground level to an upper floor, the primary reason is 'no clear way to reach that floor,' not a combination of height, obstacles, and access—unless each is independently confirmed in the scene.
- 'Available' seats, spaces, or slots means unoccupied and usable, not merely physically present. If all seats in front of a person are occupied by other people, the count of available seats is zero. Always verify occupancy status before reporting availability.
- When a question asks which road or area is obstructed, evaluate each named direction (top, bottom, left, right) independently rather than focusing only on the most visually salient crowd or blockage. Multiple roads can be obstructed simultaneously. Check every option—including compound answers like 'both X and Y'—before selecting.
- In organized environments such as warehouses with clearly visible aisles, storage yards with labeled lanes, or parking structures with marked paths, do not assume blockage without visible evidence of obstruction. If the aisles appear clear and unobstructed, the correct judgment is that direct paths to material stacks exist. Absence of visible obstacles means accessibility, not uncertainty.
- Do not over-expand a blocked zone beyond what the obstructing object physically covers. If a large object blocks a door but does not extend to the adjacent staircase, only the door is inaccessible—not both the door and the staircase. Evaluate each access point's zone independently.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- When two objects of the same category are at different depths, their apparent sizes in the image differ due to perspective projection. The object closer to the camera appears larger even if both are physically identical. Before concluding a true size difference, mentally relocate both objects to the same depth plane.
- A single pairwise size difference between two instances does not justify a global conclusion such as 'all different' or 'all same.' Aggregate the comparison across every visible instance and choose the answer that best describes the overall distribution, such as 'mostly similar.'
- Apparent occlusion of a large distant object by a small nearby object does not mean the small object could physically cover or replace the large one. A flower pot in the foreground may visually overlap a tree in the background, yet the flowers could only cover a tiny branch of that tree in reality.
- When comparing a loop, chain, ring, or surrounding structure to the object it encircles, distinguish inner radius from outer radius and from enclosing span. A chain running around the outside of a wheel has a larger effective radius than the wheel itself.
- For items displayed together in retail, craft, or decorative contexts, the most reliable size comparison is within the same design family. Cross-design comparisons can mislead because shape, color, and decoration differences bias perceived size independently of true dimensions.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Facing Direction) ---
- Facing direction is most directly revealed by an object's functional front, such as a human face, a vehicle front, a chair opening, or a readable sign surface.
- Head direction, body direction, and gaze direction are related but not identical spatial properties.
- Facial direction refers primarily to where the face is oriented, not necessarily where the torso is aligned.
- A group-level question about the most common direction requires aggregation across individuals rather than focus on the most salient person.
- People in study, work, or eating scenes often direct their faces downward toward a table, paper, or device.
- Facing toward each other is a pairwise relational pattern and does not automatically describe the dominant direction of the whole group.
- A person seen frontally is not necessarily looking toward the camera if the head is pitched downward.
- A side profile indicates that the face or front is oriented laterally relative to the camera rather than directly toward it.
- When most visible heads are tilted down, downward is a stronger descriptor than front-facing even if some torsos remain aligned with the room.
- A chair's facing direction is defined by the direction a seated person would naturally look when using it.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- Handedness depends on the geometry of grasping and use, not on whether the object appears on the left or right side of the image.
- A tool with a symmetric shaft and no side-specific handle, trigger, or guard is typically neutral with respect to left-handed or right-handed use.
- Long linear tools such as mops, brooms, poles, and sticks are usually hand-neutral unless they contain a visible asymmetric grip structure.
- A true left- or right-hand preference usually arises from asymmetric handle placement, unilateral guards, offset grips, or directional controls.
- The tilt direction of a tool does not by itself imply a preferred carrying hand.
- The fact that a tool is placed to the left of another object does not imply that it is easier to grasp with the left hand.
- When no clear side-specific affordance is visible, a neutral interpretation is more physically grounded than forcing a left or right bias.
- Questions about carrying convenience refer to the accessibility of the grasp region rather than the semantic identity of nearby objects.

80.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a room, person, or task depends on proportional compatibility, not just absolute dimensions. A chandelier that is physically large may still be too small for a grand hall, while a small lamp may be perfectly appropriate for a reading nook.
- Furniture, lighting fixtures, signs, and decorative centerpieces are normally chosen to be proportionate to the room they occupy. When a question asks whether a fixture is 'appropriate' or 'needs to be larger,' it is testing awareness of room-to-object scale matching, not just whether the fixture exists.
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- For questions about seating capacity, stacking fit, or carrying capacity of containers, reason about functional volume and arrangement geometry rather than only comparing one linear dimension such as width or height. Two medium tables together can seat as many people as one large table if each table's perimeter provides sufficient chair positions.
- When objects are stacked, their combined height may or may not match the interior height of a container. If individual items are shorter than the container, stacking more layers can eventually fill the space, but only if the question specifies enough layers. Do not assume a single additional stack equals the remaining gap.

87.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- When counting objects that block a door, exit, or path, enumerate every distinct physical item in the approach corridor individually before summing. Common blockers include hand trucks, mop buckets, brooms, cones, chairs, boxes, and cleaning equipment. Do not stop at the one or two most obvious items; systematically scan the entire access zone from left to right and floor to ceiling.
- A door's access zone extends beyond the door frame itself: it includes the full swing arc of the door panel, the corridor a person must walk through to reach the door, and any area needed to grip and pull or push the handle. Any object—even a small cone, bucket, or leaning broom—that falls within this extended zone counts as a blocker.
- For large-count obstruction questions (e.g., 10+ items blocking a door), build a structured inventory by scanning the scene in spatial order (left-to-right, bottom-to-top). Assign each visually distinct item a number. Count stacks of identical items (e.g., water bottle packs, cardboard boxes) as separate objects when they are individually distinguishable. Verify the final count against the answer options before committing.
- On multi-lane roads and highways, a vehicle in one lane does not have a direct path to vehicles in the opposing lane or on a different level if a median barrier, guardrail, jersey wall, or grade separation intervenes. 'Direct path' means continuous same-surface traversal without crossing physical dividers. Opposing traffic flow alone is insufficient to block a path, but a concrete median is.
- When identifying which specific object obstructs a target (e.g., which drawer obstructs the glasses), trace an imaginary access line from the user's approach direction to the target object. The obstructing object is the first item that intersects this access line. For vertically stacked furniture like shelves or drawer units, an open or protruding drawer on the floor above can intrude downward into the access corridor of the shelf below.
- Human unaided vertical reach is approximately 2.0–2.3 meters for an average adult. In warehouse or industrial shelving, the first (ground-level) shelf is typically reachable; shelves from the second tier upward are generally unreachable without a ladder or lift. A ceiling-mounted object such as a chandelier or overhead pipe is always inaccessible by normal human reach. When the question asks which floors are unreachable, err toward including more floors as unreachable rather than fewer.
- When a question asks about the reason for inaccessibility and multiple plausible reasons are listed, select the most specific and visually verifiable reason rather than defaulting to 'all of the above.' For example, if no staircase, ladder, or elevator is visible connecting ground level to an upper floor, the primary reason is 'no clear way to reach that floor,' not a combination of height, obstacles, and access—unless each is independently confirmed in the scene.
- 'Available' seats, spaces, or slots means unoccupied and usable, not merely physically present. If all seats in front of a person are occupied by other people, the count of available seats is zero. Always verify occupancy status before reporting availability.
- When a question asks which road or area is obstructed, evaluate each named direction (top, bottom, left, right) independently rather than focusing only on the most visually salient crowd or blockage. Multiple roads can be obstructed simultaneously. Check every option—including compound answers like 'both X and Y'—before selecting.
- In organized environments such as warehouses with clearly visible aisles, storage yards with labeled lanes, or parking structures with marked paths, do not assume blockage without visible evidence of obstruction. If the aisles appear clear and unobstructed, the correct judgment is that direct paths to material stacks exist. Absence of visible obstacles means accessibility, not uncertainty.
- Do not over-expand a blocked zone beyond what the obstructing object physically covers. If a large object blocks a door but does not extend to the adjacent staircase, only the door is inaccessible—not both the door and the staircase. Evaluate each access point's zone independently.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- When two objects of the same category are at different depths, their apparent sizes in the image differ due to perspective projection. The object closer to the camera appears larger even if both are physically identical. Before concluding a true size difference, mentally relocate both objects to the same depth plane.
- A single pairwise size difference between two instances does not justify a global conclusion such as 'all different' or 'all same.' Aggregate the comparison across every visible instance and choose the answer that best describes the overall distribution, such as 'mostly similar.'
- Apparent occlusion of a large distant object by a small nearby object does not mean the small object could physically cover or replace the large one. A flower pot in the foreground may visually overlap a tree in the background, yet the flowers could only cover a tiny branch of that tree in reality.
- When comparing a loop, chain, ring, or surrounding structure to the object it encircles, distinguish inner radius from outer radius and from enclosing span. A chain running around the outside of a wheel has a larger effective radius than the wheel itself.
- For items displayed together in retail, craft, or decorative contexts, the most reliable size comparison is within the same design family. Cross-design comparisons can mislead because shape, color, and decoration differences bias perceived size independently of true dimensions.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In everyday environments with air, air resistance significantly affects objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratio. Light, thin, and wide objects such as paper, feathers, fabric, and plastic bags fall much slower than dense, compact objects. However, this distinction only applies when the objects are clearly different in density and shape. When two objects are both dense and compact (e.g., an apple and a coin, a phone and a stone), they fall at nearly the same speed and the result depends on specific environmental conditions such as height and air currents.
- A solid, dense object such as a roll of tape, a metal can, or a ceramic mug is NOT a 'light flat object' even if it has a ring or disc shape. These objects fall quickly and directly, similar to other dense compact objects. The 'light flat object' category applies only to objects that are both lightweight AND thin, such as sheets of paper, leaves, feathers, and thin fabric.
- Liquid poured from a container follows a curved (parabolic) downward path, never a perfectly straight line. When liquid is poured onto a surface such as a cake on a plate, it flows outward and spreads across the surface it lands on.
- A round or cylindrical object can roll on a flat horizontal surface if given an initial push, even without any slope. The push provides initial kinetic energy, and the object rolls until friction brings it to a stop. On a tilted surface, a round object rolls without needing any push. The key distinction is: a slope causes continuous rolling, while a push on a flat surface causes temporary rolling that eventually stops.
- Objects with non-circular contact geometry do not roll regardless of slope or push. For example, a cup standing upright has a flat circular base and does not roll. A book lying flat does not roll. The rolling behavior depends on whether the contact patch between the object and the surface is a curved line or a flat area.
- An object may be prevented from rolling even if it has a generally cylindrical shape, when a specific feature breaks the circular symmetry of its cross-section. For example, a pen with a clip or a flat-sided cap will not roll because the clip or flat portion acts as a brake that stops the rotation.
- Static friction prevents objects from sliding or rolling on slightly tilted surfaces. When a surface is tilted slowly and gradually, objects remain stationary as long as the gravitational force component along the surface is smaller than the maximum static friction force. On rough surfaces, objects typically remain stationary up to about 15 degrees of tilt. Only when the tilt exceeds the friction threshold do objects begin to move. Therefore, slowly tilting a book with objects on it does not necessarily cause immediate movement.
- When the bottom object in a stack is QUICKLY pulled away (like a tablecloth trick), the upper objects remain momentarily in place due to inertia, then drop vertically onto the surface below. However, when the bottom object is SLOWLY removed, the upper objects gradually lose support and may slide or topple during the removal process. The inertia effect only works with rapid removal.
- An object floats in liquid when its overall average density is less than the liquid. Objects with internal air pockets tend to float even if their solid material is denser. Whole citrus fruits float due to trapped air in porous skin, but sliced fruit sinks because cutting removes air pockets.
- The force that initiates movement of a previously stable resting object is typically a specific trigger such as wind, vibration, a bump, or surface tilt — not gravity itself. Gravity is always present as a constant downward pull but does not explain why a stable object suddenly begins to move. When asked 'what force causes X to fall or move', the answer is usually the specific trigger that broke the equilibrium.
- When objects land on or fall onto elastic or soft surfaces such as sofa cushions, mattresses, trampolines, or inflatable balls, they bounce. A rigid object like a picture frame falling onto a sofa will bounce off the cushion and land on the floor nearby. A ball rolling into a cone or wall will bounce away at an angle rather than simply stopping. Bouncing is especially pronounced for rigid objects on soft surfaces and for elastic objects on hard surfaces.
- When a lightweight curtain or fabric hangs near another object such as a lamp or pole, wind blowing the fabric can cause it to reach and wrap around the nearby object. The fabric does not simply float straight outward — it moves in the wind direction and interacts with objects in its path. If a lamp is positioned between the window and the room interior, wind-blown curtains will contact the lamp before floating further into the room.
- Whether a physical obstacle can stop a moving object depends on the relative mass and speed. A light, stationary obstacle such as a comb or a thin stick can fully stop a slow-moving object, but may be knocked aside or jumped over by a fast-moving object. The correct answer for 'can X stop Y' is often 'depends on the speed' when the obstacle is significantly lighter than the moving object.
- Certain objects cannot stand upright in orientations other than their designed resting position due to their shape. Shoes, for example, have curved soles and narrow side profiles that make them impossible to balance on their sides — gravity pulls them back to their natural resting orientation (sole down or lying on their side). The inability to stand is caused by the combination of gravity and the object's geometry creating no stable equilibrium in the attempted orientation.
- When the image does not provide enough information to determine what is directly below, behind, or in the path of a falling or moving object, the correct answer is 'cannot determine from this view' rather than guessing. This applies when the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship between the moving object and potential landing targets.
- When analyzing a physical scenario, prefer the simplest and most direct outcome. If a statue is pushed gently on a shelf, it slides — do not assume it will fly off or topple dramatically. If liquid is poured onto food on a plate, it lands on the food and spreads — do not overanalyze whether it technically touches the plate first. Simple, intuitive physical outcomes are usually correct.

--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Whether an object is appropriately sized for a room, person, or task depends on proportional compatibility, not just absolute dimensions. A chandelier that is physically large may still be too small for a grand hall, while a small lamp may be perfectly appropriate for a reading nook.
- Furniture, lighting fixtures, signs, and decorative centerpieces are normally chosen to be proportionate to the room they occupy. When a question asks whether a fixture is 'appropriate' or 'needs to be larger,' it is testing awareness of room-to-object scale matching, not just whether the fixture exists.
- Text legibility on a sign or poster depends on the joint scale of the display surface and the font size as projected to the viewer's eye. Making the entire poster larger—not just walking closer or enlarging only the font—is often the correct remedy when text is too small to read at the intended viewing distance.
- For questions about seating capacity, stacking fit, or carrying capacity of containers, reason about functional volume and arrangement geometry rather than only comparing one linear dimension such as width or height. Two medium tables together can seat as many people as one large table if each table's perimeter provides sufficient chair positions.
- When objects are stacked, their combined height may or may not match the interior height of a container. If individual items are shorter than the container, stacking more layers can eventually fill the space, but only if the question specifies enough layers. Do not assume a single additional stack equals the remaining gap.

113.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.
